event: Archaic (pre-ceramic) Amerindian peoples, sometimes called the Siboney, established the earliest known settlements on Antigua, living by fishing, shellfish gathering, and stone tool use.
Ceramic-age Saladoid Arawakan-speaking peoples migrated from South America via the Lesser Antilles, introducing agriculture, pottery, and village life to Antigua and Barbuda.
Carib (Kalinago) peoples expanded through the Lesser Antilles, increasingly displacing or absorbing earlier Arawak populations across the region.
Christopher Columbus sighted Antigua during his second voyage and named it Santa Maria de la Antigua after a church in Seville.
English colonists from St. Kitts established the first permanent European settlement on Antigua, claiming it for England.
A French force briefly captured Antigua, but the island was returned to English control by the Treaty of Breda the following year.
Sir Christopher Codrington established Betty's Hope, Antigua's first large-scale sugar plantation, accelerating the shift to a sugar monoculture worked by enslaved Africans.
The Codrington family leased the island of Barbuda from the Crown, using it primarily to supply provisions and labor to their Antiguan sugar estates.
An alleged slave conspiracy led by an enslaved man known as Prince Klaas (Court) was discovered and brutally suppressed, with dozens of the accused executed.
Horatio Nelson arrived to command the British Royal Navy dockyard at English Harbour, later known as Nelson's Dockyard.
The British Parliament abolished the transatlantic slave trade, ending the legal importation of enslaved Africans into Antigua.
Slavery was fully abolished in Antigua without an apprenticeship period, immediately emancipating the enslaved population on 1 August.
A powerful earthquake struck the region, causing severe damage to buildings across Antigua including in the capital St. John's.
Barbuda was formally annexed to Antigua, uniting the two islands under a single colonial administration.
Antigua became part of the British colony of the Leeward Islands federation.
Trade unionist and political leader Vere Cornwall Bird helped found the Antigua Trades and Labour Union, galvanizing the labor and political movement.
Vere Cornwall Bird became president of the Antigua Trades and Labour Union, building the base for his decades-long political dominance.
The Antigua Labour Party was formed, growing out of the trade union movement led by Vere Bird.
Universal adult suffrage was introduced, dramatically expanding the franchise in Antigua and Barbuda.
The Leeward Islands federation was dissolved, and Antigua became a separate colony.
Antigua joined the short-lived West Indies Federation, an attempt to unite Britain's Caribbean colonies.
The West Indies Federation collapsed, ending the regional union and prompting Antigua to seek its own constitutional path.
Antigua became an associated state of the United Kingdom, gaining full internal self-government with Vere Bird as premier while Britain retained defense and foreign affairs.
The Progressive Labour Movement won the general election, briefly ousting Vere Bird's Antigua Labour Party from power.
The sugar industry, long the economic backbone of the colony, was effectively shut down as it became unprofitable.
Vere Bird's Antigua Labour Party returned to power, restoring his leadership ahead of the push for full independence.
Antigua and Barbuda gained full independence from the United Kingdom on 1 November, with Vere Cornwall Bird as the first prime minister.
Antigua and Barbuda was admitted to the United Nations on 11 November, shortly after achieving independence.
Antigua and Barbuda supported and provided a staging point for the United States-led invasion of Grenada.
A gun-smuggling scandal implicated Vere Bird Jr., a government minister and the prime minister's son, in arms trafficking linked to Colombian drug cartels.
Lester Bird succeeded his father Vere Bird as prime minister after the Antigua Labour Party won re-election, extending the family's long rule.
Hurricane Luis caused extensive destruction across Antigua and especially devastated the island of Barbuda.
Hurricane Lenny battered the islands during a damaging late-1990s hurricane season.
Baldwin Spencer's United Progressive Party defeated the Antigua Labour Party, ending the Bird family's nearly continuous half-century hold on power.
Antigua and Barbuda co-hosted matches of the ICC Cricket World Cup, opening the new Sir Vivian Richards Stadium named for the legendary Antiguan batsman.
American financier Allen Stanford, a major investor knighted in Antigua, was charged in a massive Ponzi scheme that severely damaged the country's economy.
Antigua and Barbuda won a long-running World Trade Organization dispute against the United States over online gambling restrictions, gaining authorization for trade sanctions.
Gaston Browne led the Antigua Labour Party back to power, becoming prime minister after a decisive election victory.
Hurricane Irma struck as a Category 5 storm, devastating Barbuda so severely that the entire island was evacuated for the first time in modern history.
Gaston Browne's Antigua Labour Party won re-election, securing another term to lead post-hurricane recovery efforts.
Following the death of Queen Elizabeth II, Prime Minister Gaston Browne announced plans to hold a referendum on becoming a republic within a few years.
Gaston Browne and the Antigua Labour Party won a third consecutive general election victory.
